title: What Love Is
body:
Love is one of the most studied and least neatly defined human experiences. It can refer to romantic attachment, parental care, friendship, devotion, desire, and commitment. Those forms are related, but they are not identical. Love is best understood as a cluster of bonds and motivations that push people toward closeness, care, and sustained attention to another person.

Biology matters here. Attachment systems, reward circuitry, hormones such as oxytocin, and early developmental experience all shape how love feels and how secure it becomes. But love is not only biology. It is also shaped by culture, memory, language, and moral expectations.

Different traditions have tried to sort the kinds of love more carefully. Eros, philia, storge, and agape are one classical distinction. Modern psychology adds attachment theory, while philosophy asks whether love is something we choose, something that happens to us, or both.
keyPoints: Love includes several different forms of attachment and care
It has biological, psychological, and cultural dimensions
Romantic love is not the only important kind of love
Attachment and reward systems help explain why love feels so powerful
Love is shaped by both choice and involuntary feeling
Language and culture influence how love is understood
furtherReading: 'The Four Loves' by C. S. Lewis
'Attached' by Amir Levine and Rachel Heller
'The Art of Loving' by Erich Fromm
